andrea-vance-stuff.name: Andrea Vance

connections.role: Formerly Fairfax NZ
United Future leader and Revenue Minister — Textgate 2013 (86 emails in 13 days; Dunne resigned; Vance's phone records and parliamentary swipe card data were improperly accessed)

facts.text: 'Textgate' (2013): exchanged 86 emails over 13 days with Revenue Minister Peter Dunne about GCSB leak inquiry; Dunne resigned; a subsequent inquiry improperly accessed Vance's phone records AND parliamentary swipe card movement data — a major NZ privacy scandal

andrea-vance-stuff.bio: Andrea Vance is a Northern Irish political journalist currently serving as a senior journalist at Stuff in Wellington. She grew up near Belfast, where her father edited three local newspapers. She worked for seven years as an investigative reporter at the British tabloid News of the World. When the phone hacking scandal broke, Vance was in the Glasgow newsroom and has denied any personal involvement, stating "no one had any idea it was going on." She subsequently worked as night news editor at The Scotsman before emigrating to New Zealand with her Kiwi husband. She became a prominent press gallery journalist, working for Fairfax/Stuff and later 1News. In 2013, she was at the centre of the Peter Dunne email scandal, in which minister Dunne resigned after refusing to release 86 email exchanges with Vance regarding a leaked GCSB report. Her phone records and parliamentary swipe-card data were improperly provided to the inquiry without her knowledge, prompting a major privacy breach complaint. She was named Political Journalist of the Year in 2025.
andrea-vance-stuff.methodology: Based on 10 articles scored by AI

anna-leask-nzherald.bio: Anna Leask is a senior crime and justice journalist at the NZ Herald, where she has worked since 2008. She hosts the award-winning true crime podcast 'A Moment In Crime' and is a published author with Penguin Books NZ. She has covered NZ's highest-profile criminal cases over 20 years including the March 15 terror attack, Grace Millane murder, and Pike River mine disaster.

audrey-young-nzherald.name: Audrey Young

connections.role: Political editor NZ Herald

audrey-young-nzherald.bio: Audrey Young is one of New Zealand's most experienced political journalists. She began her career as a sub-editor at the Auckland Sun, and after its closure in 1988 joined the New Zealand Herald as a sub-editor. She rose through the ranks to become the Herald's political editor, a role she held for 18 years (2003-2021), leading the paper's press gallery team in Wellington. In 2021, she transitioned to the role of Senior Political Correspondent, allowing her to focus on in-depth reporting. She was named Political Journalist of the Year at the Voyager Media Awards in 2018, 2020, and 2023. Her 30th anniversary at the Herald was acknowledged in Parliament. She has a particular interest in foreign affairs and Treaty of Waitangi issues, and served for six years as the union representative on the Press Council.
audrey-young-nzherald.methodology: Based on 1 articles scored by AI

bernard-hickey-thespinoff.name: Bernard Hickey

connections.role: Economics columnist

bernard-hickey-thespinoff.bio: Bernard Hickey is a Wellington-based financial journalist, editor, and commentator with over two decades of experience spanning Reuters, the Financial Times Group, and Fairfax Media, working across London, Singapore, and Sydney. He co-founded and edited Interest.co.nz and Newsroom Pro. He is best known for an acknowledged ideological shift over his career: initially a conventional free-market economics commentator, he moved substantially leftward, becoming a vocal critic of New Zealand's housing policies, wealth inequality, and what he characterises as the failure of neoliberal settings to deliver for younger generations. His podcast with The Spinoff, "When the Facts Change" (named after the Keynes aphorism), reflects this evolution. He founded The Kaka, a Substack newsletter covering political economy, housing, climate, and poverty.
bernard-hickey-thespinoff.methodology: Based on 85 articles scored by AI

matthew-hooton-nzherald.bio: Matthew Hooton is a New Zealand PR consultant, lobbyist, and centre-right political commentator. He founded Exceltium in 2005, providing government relations and PR to major clients. He is known for his NZ Herald column and as a panellist on RNZ's political discussions. His dual role as commentator and lobbyist has drawn persistent conflict-of-interest criticism. In 2020, he was hired as a National Party consultant after helping engineer Todd Muller's leadership coup but resigned within months. He later settled a defamation claim brought by former National MP Steven Joyce.

max-rashbrooke-stuff.bio: Max Rashbrooke (born 1980, Wellington) is a New Zealand journalist, author, and researcher best known for his work on economic inequality. He studied at Victoria University of Wellington. From 2003 to 2010 he worked as a political journalist in London, including freelancing for The Guardian. He has authored four books on inequality, including Inequality: A New Zealand Crisis (2013). He has undertaken research contracts for organisations including the Green Party. In 2024, he co-founded the Institute for Democratic and Economic Analysis (IDEA).

mike-hosking-newstalkzb.name: Mike Hosking

connections.role: Host of Mike Hosking Breakfast on Newstalk ZB

mike-hosking-newstalkzb.bio: Mike Hosking (born 1965, Dunedin) is New Zealand's most prominent radio broadcaster. He left school at 16, starting as an advertising copywriter before getting his break on Wellington's Radio Windy. He hosted Radio New Zealand's Tonight Show and Morning Report, then joined Newstalk ZB in 2008 as weekday Breakfast host — a role he still holds. On television, he co-presented TVNZ's Breakfast (1997-2005) and Seven Sharp (2014-2017). He writes a regular opinion column for the NZ Herald. Hosking is an open supporter of the centre-right National Party and is frequently critical of Labour-led governments. His career has drawn multiple successful Broadcasting Standards Authority complaints. He is married to fellow Newstalk ZB broadcaster Kate Hawkesby. A landmark 2003 privacy case involving Hosking established "the Hosking principles" in NZ common law.
